design_philosophy.core_loop: Identify targets in static/moving scenes, shoot only gangsters, avoid shooting innocent civilians and police officers; fail penalties reset progress
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Speed vs. accuracy under time pressure; distinguishing threat from non-threat before reaction window closes
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Law enforcement training simulation; skilled marksman making split-second life-or-death decisions with a light gun peripheral
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Unforgiving target discrimination mechanic: shooting innocents costs lives equally to missing gangsters. No aiming assistance or aim-help. The game treats player error as moral/tactical failure, not just mechanical failure. Grounded in real FBI training facility (Hogan's Alley range) rather than pure arcade abstraction.

difficulty.lives_system: Single life pool shared across all rounds; lose one life per mistake (shot innocent, missed gangster, or can hits ground in bonus round). Game ends at 10 misses (NES) or all lives depleted
difficulty.checkpointing: Rounds are discrete scenes; no mid-round checkpoints. Progress resets on game over
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Three selectable modes (Hogan's Alley A/wall rounds, Hogan's Alley B/town rounds, Trick Shot) allow players to choose scenarios. Difficulty escalates after each full cycle (wall + town + bonus), increasing cutout speed and complexity

progression.unlock_mechanism: Wall rounds → Town rounds → Bonus round (can shooting) → Increased speed cycle. Progression is scripted; no permanent upgrades or skill unlocks
replayability_hooks: Score-based leaderboard competition (preset thresholds award bonus lives)
Escalating difficulty cycles encourage high-score chasing
Three distinct mode variations provide different gameplay patterns
Bonus round (Trick Shot) offers different challenge type within progression
Perfect accuracy requirement creates perfectionist appeal

world_building_techniques: Contextual setting: law enforcement training scenario implied by cardboard cutout targets (gangsters, civilians, police)
Diegetic bonus round: tin cans thrown across ledges (marksmanship drill)
Real-world reference: game named after and based on actual FBI Academy Hogan's Alley training range

danger_signals: Cardboard cutout presentation (dehumanization of targets, threat classification visual language)
Light gun peripheral (authentic law enforcement training simulation tool)
Split-second shoot/don't-shoot decisions mirror real tactical scenarios
environmental_storytelling: Blank wall setting (sterile training environment, Phase 1)
City block setting (urban deployment scenario, Phase 2)
Tin can bonus round (marksmanship auxiliary drill)

sources_used: wikipedia: Hogan's Alley (video game) - Gameplay, Release, Ports, Reception, Legacy sections
